levels.title: Foundations
levels.subtitle: Sentence order, particles, everyday verbs & nouns

sections.title: Reading Korean Sentences
sections.about: How Korean sentences are built, and the formal style used throughout TOPIK I reading passages.

lessons.title: Sentence order & the polite ending -습니다
lessons.summary: Subject-Object-Verb order and the formal 하십시오체 style used in every passage.

content.html: Written Korean sentences follow Subject – Object – Verb (SOV) order, the opposite of English. The verb always comes last, and it carries the sentence's grammar: tense, politeness, and whether it's a statement or a question.

content.meaning: the verb comes last, particles mark each noun's role

content.html: TOPIK I reading passages are written in the formal style -습니다/-ㅂ니다 (statements) and -습니까?/-ㅂ니까? (questions), called 하십시오체. You'll also see the friendlier -아요/-어요 style in dialogues, messages, and postcards.

content.pattern: verb stem + -습니다 (after a consonant) / -ㅂ니다 (after a vowel)
content.meaning: formal present tense: '...(does/is)...'

content.html: Past tense adds -았/었 before -습니다: 갔습니다 (went), 먹었습니다 (ate). You'll see this often in passages describing what someone did.

Peel the ending off first. -습니다/-ㅂ니다, -았/었습니다 (past), and -(으)ㄹ 겁니다 (future) all attach to the same verb stem. Once you recognize the stem, the base meaning is easy to work out — this is the single most useful reading habit for TOPIK I.

sections.title: Particles — the Building Blocks
sections.about: The small markers attached to nouns that show their grammatical role. This is the single most-tested grammar topic in TOPIK I.

lessons.title: Subject & topic: 이/가 vs 은/는
lessons.summary: The two most common — and most confused — particles in Korean.

content.html: Korean marks a noun's grammatical role with a small particle attached right after it, instead of relying on word order alone. 이/가 and 은/는 are the two most common 'subject-like' particles, and mixing them up is the number one beginner mistake.

content.meaning: marks the grammatical subject — what performs the action, or what the sentence is newly about
marks the topic — 'as for N' — often introducing or contrasting a topic

examples.en: As for me, I am a student.

content.html: Quick test: if you could mentally add 'as for...', or you're contrasting two things, 은/는 usually fits. If you're simply stating who/what does something for the first time, 이/가 usually fits. In fill-in-the-blank questions, check the words right before and after the blank — 은/는 often sits near a contrast word like 그런데/하지만.

content.note: 저 사람 introduces who this new topic is, so 는 fits — 'as for that person, (they are) my sibling'.

lessons.title: Object and place: 을/를, 에, 에서
lessons.summary: Marking what you act on, and the difference between 에 and 에서.

content.html: After the subject/topic particles, the next most common are the object particle and two 'location' particles that TOPIK I loves to test against each other.

content.meaning: marks the direct object of the verb
marks a destination, a point in time, or a static location with 있다/없다
marks the location where an action happens

examples.en: It's far from home to school.

content.html: 에 vs 에서 is a classic TOPIK I trap. 에 pairs with 가다/오다/있다 (movement toward, or plain existence at). 에서 pairs with action verbs like 공부하다, 먹다, 만나다, 일하다 (something happening at that place). Ask yourself: is this verb an action happening at the place, or just movement toward / existence at it?

lessons.title: Also, with, whose, by, from–to
lessons.summary: 도, 와/과, 의, 으로/로, 부터~까지 — the rest of the particle set.

content.html: The remaining particles appear less often individually, but the fill-in-the-blank question mixes every particle together, so you need to recognize all of them at a glance.

content.meaning: 'also / too' — adds a noun to a previous statement

examples.en: I drink coffee. I also drink juice.

content.meaning: 'and / with' — connects two nouns or marks a companion
possessive 'N's' — often dropped in speech but still tested in reading
'by means of / toward' — transportation, tools, or direction
'from N1 to N2' — usually a time range

examples.ko: 아홉 시부터 한 시까지 수업이 있습니다.

content.html: 으로/로 rule: after a vowel or ㄹ, use 로 (버스로, 지하철로); after any other consonant, use 으로 (손으로, 걸음으로).

sections.title: Everyday Vocabulary
sections.about: Places, objects and time words that TOPIK I recycles across its fill-in-the-blank and reading questions.

lessons.title: People, places & time words
lessons.summary: The high-frequency noun set behind question 5 and many reading passages.

content.html: Question 5 in the fill-in-the-blank set — and many reading passages — test everyday nouns: places you go, things you use, common time words. There's no shortcut except building vocabulary, but TOPIK I recycles a fairly small set of high-frequency words.

These fill-in-the-blank items usually give two short sentences sharing one missing noun, e.g. '(    )에 갑니다. 머리를 자릅니다.' → 미용실 (hair salon). Read both sentences before picking an answer — the second sentence is often the real clue.

sections.title: Action Verbs
sections.about: High-frequency verbs and the present-tense conjugation patterns used to describe daily actions.

lessons.title: Common verbs & the present tense
lessons.summary: Verbs that pair with a specific noun, plus the ㄹ-irregular conjugation.

content.html: The same two-sentence-clue pattern from the vocabulary lesson applies to verbs. TOPIK I likes to test verbs that pair naturally with a specific noun: 사진을 찍다 (take a photo), 이를 닦다 (brush teeth), 편지를 쓰다 (write a letter).

content.meaning: present tense (see also the Sentence Basics lesson)

content.html: Watch for ㄹ-irregular verbs: when a verb stem ends in ㄹ, that ㄹ drops before -습니다 becomes -ㅂ니다: 살다 → 삽니다, 만들다 → 만듭니다, 열다 → 엽니다.

sections.title: Question Strategy: What Is It About?
sections.about: A dedicated look at questions 1–3, the very first question type on the real exam.

lessons.title: Finding the topic word (Q1–3 strategy)
lessons.summary: Spot the category a short passage belongs to, without translating every word.

content.html: Questions 1–3 give a very short passage (1–2 sentences) and ask 무엇에 대한 이야기입니까? — 'What is this story about?'. The four answer choices are always single topic nouns like 날씨, 직업, 가족, 취미. You are not translating the sentence — you are finding its category.

Strategy: underline every content word (ignore the particles), then ask what category links them. If a passage mentions 회사원 and 의사, both are jobs → 직업. If it mentions 덥다 and 수영하다, both relate to summer → 날씨/여름.

content.box: 저는 매일 아침 운동을 합니다. 그리고 야채와 과일을 많이 먹습니다.
content.note: Exercising and eating vegetables/fruit are both about staying healthy → 건강 (health).

content.html: Common topic-word answer choices worth memorizing: 날씨(weather), 계절(season), 직업(job), 취미(hobby), 가족(family), 건강(health), 여행(travel), 나라(country), 요일(day of the week), 나이(age), 값/가격(price).

sections.title: Describing Things
sections.about: Korean adjectives conjugate like verbs, plus the adverbs that describe how, how much, and when.

lessons.title: Descriptive verbs (형용사)
lessons.summary: Korean adjectives take -습니다 directly — no separate 'to be' word.

content.html: Korean adjectives (형용사, 'descriptive verbs') conjugate exactly like action verbs — they take -습니다/-ㅂ니다 directly, with no separate 'to be' word. This trips up many learners coming from English.

Many adjectives ending in -ㅂ다 are ㅂ-irregular in casual speech (어렵다 → 어려워요), but in the formal -습니다 style used in TOPIK I reading, the ㅂ stays: 어렵다 → 어렵습니다. You can usually attach -습니다 directly to the dictionary stem minus 다.

These blanks always give two clue sentences, the second explaining a consequence of the missing adjective: '가방이 (    ). 혼자 들 수 없습니다.' (The bag is ___. I can't carry it alone.) → 무겁습니다 (heavy). Read the consequence sentence first — it usually narrows the answer to one obvious adjective.

lessons.title: Adverbs: how, how much, when
lessons.summary: Degree, manner and time adverbs that sit right before the verb.

content.html: Adverbs modify the verb and usually sit right before it. TOPIK I tests adverbs of degree (much / a lot), manner (together, alone, fast) and time (early, late).

Cause-and-effect clue sentences work here too: '한국어를 (    ) 공부했습니다. 그래서 시험을 잘 봤습니다.' (Studied Korean ___. So did well on the exam.) → 열심히 (diligently). The 그래서 (so / therefore) tells you the blank must logically explain the good result.

sections.title: Connecting Ideas
sections.about: -고 싶다 for wants, and the connective endings that join two clauses into one sentence.

lessons.title: Wanting to do something: -고 싶다
lessons.summary: One of the most frequently tested single grammar patterns in TOPIK I.

content.html: -고 싶다 attaches to a verb stem to mean 'want to ~'. It's one of the most frequently tested patterns in the fill-in-the-blank section, because every item in this pool follows the exact same shape.

content.pattern: verb stem + -고 싶다 (+ -습니다 for formal style)
content.meaning: 'want to (do)'

examples.en: I want to go to my hometown.
I want to watch a movie.

content.meaning: used when the subject is someone else, not 'I'

examples.en: My sibling wants to eat ice cream.

content.html: In this pool, the blank is always the verb stem itself (마시다, 자다, 만나다...), with 싶다 already printed after it. Read the first clue sentence for the situation, then pick the verb whose action fits: '배가 고픕니다. 밥을 (    ) 싶습니다.' (Hungry. Want to ___ rice.) → 먹고.

lessons.title: Connecting two clauses in one sentence
lessons.summary: -고, -아서/어서, -지만, -(으)면 — the connectives behind every longer passage from here on.

content.html: From Level 2 onward, passages combine two clauses into one sentence using a connective ending attached to the first verb. Recognizing these endings is essential for the ordering and comprehension questions later in this level.

content.meaning: 'and (then)' — simply lists two actions or facts in sequence

examples.en: I eat breakfast and (then) go to school.

content.meaning: 'because / so that' — the first clause is the reason for the second

examples.en: My stomach hurt, so I went to the hospital.

content.meaning: 'but' — contrasts the two clauses

examples.en: It's expensive, but I buy it.

content.meaning: 'if' — the first clause is a condition for the second

examples.en: If you have time, let's meet.

content.html: Between separate sentences (not inside one), the same logic is carried by connecting adverbs: 그리고 (and), 그래서 (so), 그런데/하지만 (but), 그러면 (if so). You'll meet these again in the sentence-ordering lesson.

sections.title: Practical & Everyday Texts
sections.about: Reading notices, ads and messages, and the 'find the one that's NOT correct' question type.

lessons.title: Notices, ads & the 'NOT correct' question
lessons.summary: Three choices are true, one is false — usually by a single changed detail.

content.html: Questions 10–12 show a short practical text — a for-sale post, a schedule, an invitation, a label — and ask you to find the ONE choice that does NOT match it. Three choices are true; one is false, often by a single changed detail.

Strategy: read the source text once for its facts (numbers, dates, prices, condition, contact method), then check each choice against those facts one at a time. The wrong choice usually flips a number, negates a fact, or swaps who does what.

content.box:
한국어 교실
일시: 5월 3일 토요일 오후 2시
장소: 3층 세미나실
신청: 이메일(class@hanguk.com)
content.note: The notice says to apply by email, not by phone — that's the mismatched detail.

content.html: Common text types for this question: 판매 글 (for-sale post), 안내문 (notice/announcement), 초대장 (invitation), 상품 라벨/사용 설명서 (product label / instructions). Numbers and contact methods (이메일/전화/방문) are the details most often altered.

sections.title: Comprehension Strategies
sections.about: Matching content by elimination, and identifying a passage's central idea.

lessons.title: Matching content: scanning for facts
lessons.summary: The mirror image of the NOT-correct question — eliminate what contradicts the text.

content.html: Questions 13–15 are the mirror image of the NOT-correct question: now three choices are false and only ONE matches the passage. The passage is usually 2–4 plain sentences about a daily routine or simple situation.

Because only one answer is true, eliminate rather than confirm: cross out any choice that contradicts a specific fact in the text (wrong day, wrong place, wrong person, opposite action). The last choice standing is your answer — verify it directly matches the text before choosing it.

content.box: 저는 아침에 커피를 마십니다. 회사에서 점심을 먹습니다. 저녁에는 집에서 텔레비전을 봅니다.
content.note: Only the third choice states a fact written directly in the passage; the others change the time, place, or action.

lessons.title: Finding the main idea
lessons.summary: The writer's takeaway, usually signaled near the last sentence.

content.html: Questions 16–18 ask for the 중심 생각 (central idea) of a short passage — usually three sentences that build toward one opinion or conclusion, often stated in the last sentence.

Look for an opinion or conclusion marker near the end: 그래서 저는 ~것이 좋다고 생각합니다 (so I think ~ is good), 제일 좋습니다 (is the best), -어야 합니다 (must/should). The main idea is rarely just a fact repeated from the first sentence — it's the writer's takeaway.

content.box: 저는 지하철로 회사에 갑니다. 지하철은 빠르고 길이 막히지 않습니다. 그래서 저는 지하철이 제일 좋습니다.
content.note: The final opinion (지하철이 제일 좋다) is supported by 빠르고 길이 막히지 않는다 — summarized as 'fast and convenient'.

sections.title: Sentence Ordering
sections.about: Reconstructing a scrambled paragraph using the connectives from this level.

lessons.title: Ordering sentences with discourse markers
lessons.summary: Find the sentence with nothing to refer back to — it comes first.

content.html: Questions 27–28 give four labeled sentences (가/나/다/라) in scrambled order. Your job is to reconstruct the original, logical sequence — this directly tests the connecting words from the previous grammar lesson.

Step 1: find the sentence that must come first — usually the one with no connective or pronoun referring back to something ('저는 지난 주말에 책을 샀습니다', a plain new statement). Step 2: chain the rest using their connectives — 그래서/그런데/그리고 signal what kind of sentence comes next, and words like 그 (그것/그 책) point back to something already mentioned.

content.box:
(가) 저는 지난 주말에 책을 한 권 샀습니다.
(나) 그 책은 요리에 관한 책입니다.
(다) 그래서 요즘 집에서 요리 연습을 합니다.
(라) 책에는 쉬운 요리법이 많이 있습니다.
content.note: Bought a book → that book is about cooking → the book has many easy recipes → so, as a result, practicing cooking at home. (다) comes last because 그래서 announces a result, and (라) explains 책 (introduced in 나) before that result.

content.html: 가 always opens the first sentence in this question type — you never need to decide which sentence starts the paragraph, only how 나/다/라 fit around it.

sections.title: Paired Passage Reading (Q19–40)
sections.about: One shared passage, two questions — the format that fills the second half of the TOPIK I reading section.

lessons.title: Blank (㉠) + content match — the paired strategy
lessons.summary: Read locally for the blank, then eliminate for the content question.

content.html: From question 19 onward, TOPIK I moves to paired questions: one shared passage, two questions. The first is almost always a blank marked ㉠ (sometimes ㉡) inside the passage; the second asks you to find the statement that matches the passage's content (같은 것을 고르십시오) or, occasionally, its purpose or something you can infer.

For the ㉠ blank question: read only the sentence right around ㉠ — you rarely need the whole passage. For the content-matching question: reuse the elimination technique from the Matching Content lesson, now applied to a longer passage. Read once for the gist, then verify each choice sentence-by-sentence.

content.box: 지난달에 새 휴대폰을 샀습니다. ( ㉠ ) 사진을 찍는 기능이 좋아서 이 휴대폰을 선택했습니다. 요즘 이 휴대폰으로 사진을 많이 찍습니다.
content.note: ㉠ needs a sentence that bridges 'bought a new phone' and 'chose it for its camera' — 'it was also very cheap' fits as an added reason. Any choice introducing an unrelated new fact breaks the flow.

content.html: As the pair numbers increase (19 → 39 on the real exam), passages get longer and the second question sometimes changes shape: 왜 이 글을 썼습니까? (why was this written — purpose) or 알 수 있는 것을 고르십시오 (what can be inferred). The elimination method still applies — just be ready for 'why / purpose' instead of a plain fact.

levels.title: Intermediate Reading & Writing I
levels.subtitle: Grammar connectives, practical texts & your first writing task

sections.title: Intermediate Connectives
sections.about: Grammar patterns marking reason, contrast and background beyond Level 1–2 (-느라고, -는 바람에, -더라도...).

lessons.title: Reason, contrast & progress connectives
lessons.summary: -느라고, -는 바람에/통에, -더라도, -(으)ㄹ 때마다, -아/어 가다, -(으)ㄹ 뻔하다.

content.html: TOPIK II connects two clauses with more nuanced logic than Level 1–2's -아서/어서, -지만, -(으)면. The patterns below each add a specific shade of meaning: an unwanted side effect, a narrow miss, a gradual change.

content.meaning: 'because (I was busy) doing ~' — the ongoing action caused a negative or burdensome result; same subject in both clauses, never used with a past-tense clause on -느라고 itself

examples.en: I couldn't sleep because I was studying for the exam.
I didn't hear it because I was answering the phone.

content.html: -느라고 vs the Level 2 -아서/어서: -느라고 only works when (1) both clauses share the same subject and (2) the second clause is a negative or burdensome result (couldn't do something, was late, got tired). Plain -아서/어서 has no such restriction — it works for any reason, positive or negative, even with different subjects.

content.meaning: 'because of ~' — an unexpected, uncontrollable event caused a negative result

examples.ko: 갑자기 비가 오는 바람에 소풍이 취소되었다.
examples.en: The picnic was cancelled because it suddenly started raining.
With the child crying, everything was chaotic.

content.meaning: 'even if / even though' — a stronger, more hypothetical concession than -아도/어도

examples.ko: 내일 눈이 오더라도 여행은 갈 거예요.
examples.en: Even if it snows tomorrow, I'll still go on the trip.

content.meaning: 'every time / whenever ~'

examples.ko: 이 노래를 들을 때마다 고향이 생각나요.
examples.en: Every time I hear this song, I think of my hometown.

content.meaning: gradual change moving from now toward completion or a future state (pairs with -아/어 오다 for change up to now)

examples.en: I'm gradually getting used to life in Korea.

content.meaning: 'almost did ~ (but didn't)' — a narrowly avoided event, always past tense: -(으)ㄹ 뻔했다

examples.en: Traffic was bad, so I almost missed the flight.

content.html: In the fill-in-the-blank question, the two surrounding clauses tell you which shade of meaning fits: a negative surprise → -는 바람에/통에; a busy cause with an inconvenient result → -느라고; a narrow miss → -(으)ㄹ 뻔하다. If more than one choice seems grammatically possible, check whether the situation is truly unexpected (바람에/통에) or simply causal (느라고, 아서).

sections.title: Similar Meaning Expressions
sections.about: Choosing the paraphrase closest in meaning to an underlined grammar pattern.

lessons.title: Choosing the closest paraphrase
lessons.summary: Two sub-types: grammar-pattern synonyms and idiom/vocabulary synonyms.

content.html: Questions 7–8 underline part of a sentence and ask you to choose the option closest in meaning. There are two flavors: a grammar pattern that has a near-synonym pattern (e.g. -자마자 ≈ -기가 무섭게), or an idiom / set expression whose plain-language equivalent you simply need to know (e.g. 씻은 듯이 = 완전히).

For grammar-pattern synonyms: reason the meaning out from the pattern itself, the same way you learned each pattern. For idioms: there's no shortcut — build a small personal list as you meet them, since the exam draws from a fairly limited, recurring set of everyday idioms.

words.en: = 아는 사람이 많다 (to know a lot of people)

content.note: 깜짝 놀라다 is a set phrase meaning 'to be very startled/surprised' — the plain paraphrase is simply 매우 놀랐다.

content.html: Eliminate choices that reverse the meaning (전혀 놀라지 않았다 = 'not surprised at all', the opposite) or introduce an unrelated feeling (실망하다 'disappointed', 궁금하다 'curious') not present in the original at all.

sections.title: Short Informational Texts
sections.about: Ads, notices and short articles — identifying what a text is about.

lessons.title: Decoding advertising slogans
lessons.summary: The harder sibling of the Level 1 topic-finding strategy — now in catchy, indirect ad-copy language.

content.html: Questions 9–12 show a short slogan-style text — an ad, a public campaign, or a simple notice — and ask what it's about. This is the same skill as Level 1's 'What is it about?' strategy, but the language is now indirect: wordplay, rhythm, and rhetorical questions replace plain description.

Strategy: ignore the catchy phrasing and hunt for the one or two concrete nouns or verbs hiding inside it. '발끝까지 편안하게, 하루 종일 걸어도 피곤하지 않습니다' repeats 발 (foot) and 걷다 (walk) → 신발 (shoes). If no concrete noun appears, the text is likely a public-service campaign (공익 광고) — match it to its theme instead of a product.

content.box:
쿵쿵 뛰는 발소리, 아랫집에는 천둥소리!
실내에서는 사뿐사뿐 걸어 주세요.
content.note: '쿵쿵'(loud stomping) and '천둥소리'(thunder — how it sounds to the downstairs neighbor) both point to noise → 소음 방지 (noise prevention), a classic apartment-living PSA theme.

content.html: The eight campaign themes above cover the large majority of public-service items in this pool — memorizing them as a closed set is often faster than reasoning out the wordplay from scratch.

sections.title: Matching Notices, Graphs & Texts
sections.about: Reading a notice, a graph/chart, or a short passage and matching it to a true statement.

lessons.title: Reading notices, graphs and short articles
lessons.summary: The Level 2 matching-content strategy, applied to three formats: notice, statistics, and narrative.

content.html: Questions 13–16 reuse the matching-content skill from Level 2, now across three formats in a row: 13 is a formal notice (안내문), 14 is a graph or survey data, and 15–16 are short narrative texts — a news item or a personal story.

Notices (13): they're bullet-point lists, so most wrong choices misstate a date, time, place, or instruction. Read every bullet once, then check each choice against the exact bullet it corresponds to — don't rely on memory for numbers.

Graphs (14): first identify what's being ranked or how it changes over time. Watch for choices that swap the ranking order (e.g. claim the 2nd-highest value is actually highest), invent a number not shown, or describe a trend (increasing/decreasing) that doesn't match the data.

content.box:
[인주시 1인 가구 수 변화]

- 2014년: 8만 가구
- 2019년: 12만 가구
- 2024년: 17만 가구
content.note: The numbers rise every period (8만 → 12만 → 17만) — a steady increase, so only the first choice matches; the others invent a drop, an inflated number, and 'no change'.

content.html: Short texts (15–16): these read like a mini news story or anecdote, often with a twist in the final sentence (a surprising outcome, a change of plan). The correct answer is usually the specific, secondary detail near the end — the same elimination method from Level 2 still applies, just over more sentences.

sections.title: Writing — Sentence Completion (문제 51)
sections.about: Filling two blanks in a practical text such as an email or message with natural, appropriately formal sentences.

lessons.title: Filling two blanks in a practical text
lessons.summary: Match the register to the text type, then write one natural sentence for ㉠ and one for ㉡.

content.html: Question 51 gives a practical text — an email, a posted notice, a club recruitment flyer — with two blanks marked ㉠ and ㉡. Write exactly one natural sentence for each (10 points each). You are not translating; you are producing the sentence a Korean writer would actually use in that situation.

Identify the text type first, because it sets the register for both blanks: an email to a professor or someone senior needs full honorific style (교수님께, -습니다, -아/어 주시기 바랍니다); a public notice or club flyer stays formal but impersonal (-습니다, -아/어 주시기 바랍니다); a complaint or inquiry email is polite and direct (문의드립니다, 궁금합니다).

content.meaning: a polite written request — very common for the second blank in notices and emails

examples.ko: 필요한 물을 미리 받아 놓으시기 바랍니다.
examples.en: Please draw the water you need in advance.

content.meaning: stating an intention or purpose politely — common for opening or introducing the first blank

examples.en: We would like to recruit new members.

content.html: Read the sentence right before and after each blank — they constrain what you can write far more than the topic alone. A sentence starting with 그러므로/그래서 (therefore) right after a blank means that blank must state a fact or problem for the request to logically respond to.

content.box:
[안내문]

인주 도서관을 이용해 주셔서 감사합니다.
다음 달부터 시설 공사로 인해 3층 열람실을 (        ㉠        ).
공사 기간에는 2층 열람실을 (        ㉡        ).
이용에 불편을 드려서 죄송합니다.
model: 이용하실 수 없습니다 / 이용할 수 없습니다
이용해 주시기 바랍니다 / 이용해 주시기를 부탁드립니다
content.note: ㉠ states the problem the notice exists to announce, in plain formal style. ㉡ then gives the alternative instruction with the -아/어 주시기 바랍니다 pattern from above.

levels.title: Intermediate Reading & Writing II
levels.subtitle: Discourse flow, paired passages & describing information in writing

sections.title: Paragraph-Level Ordering
sections.about: Arranging four sentences into a coherent paragraph, one step up from the Level 2 ordering question.

sections.title: Longer Fill-in-the-Blank Passages
sections.about: Choosing the correct expression within a full paragraph of context.

sections.title: Paired Passages A–C
sections.about: Three paired-question passage styles: idiom/expression blanks, feeling & content, and blank & theme.

sections.title: Writing — Sentence Completion (문제 52)
sections.about: A second sentence-blank writing task, often explaining a general fact or comparison.

levels.title: Advanced Reading & Writing I
levels.subtitle: News headlines, long passages & describing survey data

sections.title: News Headlines
sections.about: Decoding the compressed, idiomatic language of Korean newspaper headlines.

sections.title: Long-Passage Blanks
sections.about: Fill-in-the-blank questions embedded in denser, more abstract passages.

sections.title: Content Matching (Advanced)
sections.about: Applying the matching-content strategy to longer, more nuanced passages.

sections.title: Passage Themes
sections.about: Identifying the central theme of an argumentative or explanatory passage.

sections.title: Writing — Describing Survey Data (문제 53)
sections.about: Turning a chart or survey results into a structured 200–300 character report.

levels.title: Advanced Reading & Writing II
levels.subtitle: Sentence insertion, literary & academic passages, and the final essay

sections.title: Sentence Insertion
sections.about: Finding where a missing sentence best fits back into a passage.

sections.title: Literary Passages
sections.about: Reading fiction excerpts for a character's feelings, attitude and inference.

sections.title: Academic & Argumentative Passages
sections.about: Denser passages testing purpose, blanks and content together.

sections.title: Writing — The Essay (문제 54)
sections.about: Structuring a 600–700 character opinion essay: introduction, body and conclusion.
